title: The Effect of Political Frictions on the Pricing and Supply of Insurance
abstract: Political frictions significantly affect both pricing and supply in the long-term care insurance (LTCI) market. Comparing the same insurer’s requests submitted for the same policy at the same time to different state regulators, we find that they are 13% more likely to be approved and receive 4% more of the requested amount after an election year. Over time, regulatory pushback on premium increase requests leads to persistently lower cash reserves and increases the probability of company dropout. An insurer who receives one-standard-deviation less of their requested increase is 20% more likely to leave the market next year.

article.1 Related Literature:
Editor: Ralph Koijen Ralph Koijen Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

When we model insurance markets, regulators are usually absent, or they are assumed to set prices at actuarially fair values. Yet in practice, insurance premiums are tightly governed by each state’s insurance commissioner, who must approve prices on new contracts as well as any changes to prices on existing contracts. AsFigure 1shows, similarly sized premium increase requests from the same company (New York Life Insurance) can result in very different pricing outcomes across states depending on regulators’ decisions. Hence, to understand market prices, we must also understand regulators’ incentives and whether they include nonactuarial considerations, such as local politics.

In panel A, the average premium increase requested is shown in blue across states, ranging from 20% (dark blue) to 18.7% (light blue). In panel B, the actual premium increase granted by the state regulator is shown in green across states, ranging from 30% (dark green) to 0% (pale green). In this paper, we study the long-term care insurance (LTCI) market and find strong evidence that insurance regulators are influenced by several political factors, including election cycles, party affiliation, and campaign financing. These political frictions not only affect premium changes but also ultimately affect insurers’ cash flows and the decision to supply insurance.

The private LTCI market is an ideal setting to study frictions in price adjustments, because LTCI is a relatively novel product with ex ante unknown pricing parameters. LTCI was first created in the 1980s to help Americans finance long-term care (LTC), a service that supports individuals who need assistance with Activities of Daily Living (ADLs), such as eating, showering, and dressing. Roughly 66% of Americans over age 65 are expected to need LTC, but the cost of these services is high and steadily rising (Nordman 2016). Public financing options, such as Medicare and Medicaid, are limited, so LTCI is an economically important product, providing a financial safety net for millions of individuals who may need to pay for the high cost of LTC. Unfortunately, today, the LTCI market has both rising policy prices and falling insurance supply. Our paper explores a novel factor that may explain cross-sectional variation in these market trends: political frictions due to the state regulator.

In our first and main set of analyses, we examine nearly identical premium increase requests submitted by the same company for the same policy in the same year to different state regulators. By matching the exact policy, year, and size of the request, we control for many otherwise unobservable factors, such as consumer characteristics and benefits covered, that influence regulator decisions. This allows us to instead focus on nonactuarial factors, such as political incentives, that can also sway the regulator’s verdict.

We start by examining the effect of election cycles on matched applications. We hypothesize that the state regulator is more hesitant to grant premium increases right before an election, since price increases often result in negative press and are generally unfavorable to voters. In addition, the regulator may delay their decision, because they need to focus on election campaigns. We find that relative to the year before an election, the regulator is 13% more likely to approve an increase in the year following election. Additionally, the average size of the approved increase is 4.1% more of the requested amount. Not all regulators feel the same amount of election cycle pressure, however. We find that regulators with more political capital (as proxied for by a higher winning vote margin or longer tenure length) are on average less influenced by election cycles.

We then examine two other factors that affect the insurance regulator: political affiliation and campaign financing. Comparing regulators with different political affiliations, we find that Democrats are significantly less likely to grant premium increase requests than Republicans, and they also grant less of the requested increase on average. To address the concern that political affiliations are correlated with omitted pricing factors, such as consumer demographics, we look at switches in political party following close races. We find consistent results. Shortly after a state switches to a Republican regulator in a close race, its premium requests are 58.9% more likely to be approved, and the approved increases are 39.3% more of the requested amount.

In addition to party affiliation, regulators also differ in their financial resources. We find that regulators are tougher on premium requests if they have a bigger stock of existing contributions, which reduces their need to raise additional funding from the industry. A regulator who has US$100,000 more in her stock of funding is 5.4% less likely to approve an application and approves 2.6% less of the requested amount on average. In contrast, regulators are more lenient on requests during years when they also receive a greater inflow of new contributions. One possible explanation, consistent with these findings, is that regulators may receive more donations from insurance companies when they grant more premium increases. Although some anecdotal evidence supports this, we unfortunately cannot directly test this explanation using our data set.

Next, we switch from examining individual premium increase applications to studying the marketwide effects of regulators’ decisions. We first find that election cycles can significantly explain aggregate premium patterns in the LTCI market across states and over time. Compared to the year before an election, average premium changes in a state are 0.87-percentage-points (abbreviated as pp) larger in the year following an election. This difference represents a 21.0% increase from the unconditional average of 4.1 pp.

Finally, to understand the impact of pricing frictions on supply, we study the relationship between regulator behavior and LTCI insurer exits. If frictions to insurance regulation persist over time, profit shortfalls can accumulate, driving companies to exit the market. Consistent with this theory, we find that states with regulators who are tougher on premium increases have lower cash flows and a higher probability of company dropout. Insurers governed by Democratic regulators, who tend to be tougher on increases, accumulate only 60% of the cash flows of companies governed by Republican regulators. Over the 14 years in our sample, this accumulates to a $4 billion gap in cash flows, and unsurprisingly, companies are 10.3pp more likely to drop out of the market under a Democratic regulator. Since lower cash flows over time lead to lower cash reserves, LTCI companies with tougher regulators bear greater financial risk if they choose to stay in the market and weather future cost shocks.

Ultimately, novel financial products like LTCI have the potential to improve social welfare, but they also contain unforeseen cost shocks and can be difficult to price accurately. This paper shows that political frictions of the regulator can significantly change the size of ex post pricing adjustments, affecting both cash flows and product supply over time. If we were to ignore these frictions, we would overlook a key driver of pricing heterogeneity across states and ultimately underestimate the drop in insurance supply. Examining the cause of these frictions naturally lead to policy recommendations. For example, the NAIC could appoint one rotating committee of insurance regulators, who have staggered appointments that are not tied to any particular election cycle. While our paper focuses on the LTCI market, its lessons shed light on the regulation of other novel financial products with pricing uncertainty, such as healthcare plans under the Affordable Care Act (ACA) and hybrid annuity products.
Our paper relates to three strands of literature: the political economy of financial markets, the decline of private LTCI, and general regulatory frictions in finance. To our knowledge, our paper is the first to demonstrate that political frictions influence both insurance market prices and supply in the context of the LTCI market.

First, we extend the rich literature on election cycles and political economy to the LTCI market. The broader political economy literature shows that public officials deliver positive news to increase popularity around election year (MacRae 1977;Nordhaus 1975;Rogoff and Sibert 1988). In the banking sector,Brown and Dinc (2005)andLiu and Ngo (2014)show that regulators delay intervention for failing banks until after election, perhaps to avoid unfavorable voter opinions. Although a few studies have shown that regulators’ election cycles affect insurance market outcomes (Grace and Phillips 2008;Leverty and Grace 2018), this area remains relatively understudied.

We also add to a rich literature exploring the causes behind high prices and declining supply in the private LTCI market. One branch of this literature shows that the LTCI market is adversely selected. For example,Brown, Goda, and McGarry (2012),Coe, Goda, and Van Houtven 2020,Ko (2016),Mommaerts (2015), andOster et al. (2010)demonstrate that individuals who are more likely to use nursing homes are also more likely to purchase LTCI. More specifically,Hendren (2013)shows that the market cannot exist for the highest-cost individuals. Another branch of this literature shows that demand for the product itself is low (Brown and Finkelstein 2007;Ameriks et al. 2020). This may be due to Medicaid crowding out private LTCI (Braun, Kopecky, and Koreshkova 2017;Brown and Finkelstein 2008), the undervaluation of bequests that lead consumers to spend down their savings (Lockwood 2018), or the consumer’s ability to fund LTC using home equity (Achou 2018;Boyer et al. 2017;Davidoff 2010).

Our paper is one of only a few to focus on supply-driven issues in the LTCI market (seeAmeriks et al. 2016;Braun, Kopecky, and Koreshkova 2017). Most closely related to our paper,Brown and Finkelstein (2007)shows that policy premiums were marked up above actuarial values based on benefits and ex ante utilization rates. In spite of this, actuarial and regulatory reports have found underestimated morbidity rates and overestimated lapse rates in the LTCI market (Eaton 2016;Nordman 2016;Rubin et al. 2014). While we cannot say if LTCI was mispriced ex ante, we find large and significant cross-sectional variation in insurers’ cash flows and dropout rates ex post. We find that the political environment surrounding the state regulators can help explain this variation.

Finally, we add to the discussion of how regulation itself may cause frictions in financial markets, especially the LTCI market, where product pricing underwent great changes over the last two decades. In the banking sector, several papers have shown that regulators cause frictions in bank performance (Agarwal et al. 2014;Hirtle, Kovner, and Plosser 2020;Kara and Yook 2019). In the credit card market, which also has limited pricing features and asymmetric information,Nelson (2022)shows that limitations on companies’ ability to raise rates have a significant impact on ex post pricing. In the insurance market, which is also the focus of this paper,Becker and Ivashina (2015)finds that capital requirements create incentivizes for insurers who reach for yield;Koijen and Yogo (2015)shows that statutory regulations caused life insurers to sell policies at steep discounts; andOh, Sen, and Tenekedjieva (2022)finds that stringent regulation leads to a decoupling of insurance prices from underlying risk.
article.2 Background:
2.1 The LTCI market

Private LTCI was created in the 1980s to provide financial assistance for the high cost of LTC services. Its contracts are guaranteed renewable, which means that as long as the consumer continues to make annual premium payments, the LTCI company must honor its contract. Under the contract, companies reimburse payments used to cover the costs of LTC once certain conditions are met, such as the customer’s inability to perform two or more ADLs. Premiums are set at the beginning of the contract, but they may change at any time subject to demonstrated need and regulatory approval (see Section 2.2).

Recently, the LTCI market has experienced rising premiums as well as declining supply. The number of both new policy holders and active companies has steadily declined between 1995 and 2015. While roughly 100 companies were in the market in the early 2000s, only about 40 remain today. On the other hand, annual premiums have been steadily trending up over the same period. While the gross annual premium was about $1,020 in 1995, it is a little above $1,500 in 2015.1Overall, these trends suggest significant frictions in the LTCI market have caused it to shrink over time.

Importantly, LTCI products are difficult to price due to the long lifespan of its policies and high uncertainty about future payouts. To lock in low premiums, the typical LTCI consumer enters the policy contract and starts paying annual premiums around middle age, but they only begin collecting claims 20 or 30 years later. Unlike life insurance, many LTCI products were initially uncapped, so the maximum payout of the policy is unknown at the time of purchase. Furthermore, since the 1980s, several pricing assumptions have been shown to be wrong. These include interest rate forecasts, assumed lapse rates (the rate at which customers stop paying premiums), and expected mortality rates. In premium increase requests submitted to regulators, LTCI companies have argued that many pricing assumptions have been shown to be incorrect, and thus, LTCI policies are mispriced ex post.

2.2 The evolution of LTCI premiums

Over the last 30 years, most LTCI companies have argued that policy premiums were initially priced too low. However, their ability to raise premiums differ depending on the state of operation. AsFigure 1shows, in 2013, New York Life Insurance requested premium increases of roughly 20% for the LTCI policies that it sold in all 50 states. Despite nearly identical requests, however, the premium increases that it received varied greatly, ranging from a 30% increase granted by New Hampshire to rejections from California, Connecticut, Rhode Island, and Vermont. This variation is directly caused by the state regulators, who decide whether to approve premium increase requests as well as determine the magnitude of the approved increases (Brewster and Gutterman 2014). Since regulators have an implicit obligation to protect their constituents, which include the consumers of LTCI, they may be reluctant to allow large premium increases. Their incentives may vary, however, depending on political considerations, such as their political capital and the timing of local election cycles.

Anecdotally, industry leaders have argued that some state regulators are too hesitant to allow price increases. Genworth, one of the largest LTCI providers, has stated that “[the] Massachusetts [regulator] lags behind virtually every other state in taking timely action in response to rate increase filings and in granting necessary rate increases” (Bartlett 2017). Furthermore, in its annual 10-Q statement, Genworth makes it clear that regulatory frictions in price setting have led to its dropout from specific states, including Massachusetts: “We have suspended sales in Hawaii, Massachusetts, New Hampshire, and Vermont, and will consider similar actions … in other states where we are unable to make satisfactory rate increases … .” (Genworth Financial 2018). Supporting these claims, in a survey of 26 LTCI carriers,Gordon and Pahl (2016)find that 84% of rate increase rejections from 2013 to 2016 were due to politically motivated caps or nonactuarial reasons (e.g., requested increase is “unreasonable”).

When a company submits a premium increase request,2it must provide historical evidence that costs were higher than expected.Figure 2plots the actual incurred claims (x-axis) versus the expected incurred claims (y-axis) as reported by the 15 largest LTCI companies to the NAIC. Since most companies lie to the right of the 45-degree line, the points along which projections are 100% accurate, this shows that ex post costs were generally higher than expectations. Without knowing insurers’ exact pricing assumptions, we cannot say whether these policies were ex ante mispriced; yet, even within the same insurance company, we can observe a sizable amount of variation in cash reserves and dropout decisions across states. Systematically examining thousands of requests from insurers to increase their LTCI premiums over the last 20 years, we find that these observed patterns of insurer supply may be partially explained by the nonactuarial, political incentives of state regulators, who control LTCI companies’ ability to raise prices.

The premiums data come from the NAIC Long-term Care Insurance Experience Guide, and these data cover the years 1997–2015. We observe one observation per company, state, and year. The application data come from the California Long-term Care Rate and History Guide, which covers the years 2007–2017. It has one observation per policy, company, state, and year. Finally, company dropout data are proxied for using advertisement filings from the SERFF (The System for Electronic Rates & Forms Filing) online database. In states in which insurance companies are required to file for advertising in order to sell a product, we count a company as dropped in the year that they stop filing for advertising. For each state and company, the dummy variable for dropped is equal to one if the company dropped out of the state at any point in our sample. The final dropout data cover the years 2009–2014.

First, we use novel data on individual premium increase requests submitted to insurance regulators and their corresponding regulator responses from the 2017 edition of the California Long-term Care Rate and History Guide. For any company that has ever sold LTCI in California over the past 10 years, this data set contains a detailed history of itsnationwiderate requests and regulator responses between 2007 and 2017.3This application data provides one observation per policy, company, state, and year. Summary statistics for this data set are provided in panel A ofTable 1, and histograms of both application rates and approval rates over the regulator’s election cycle are shown inFigure 3.

In the premium applications data, we observe a total of 32,098 different applications for premium increases across all 50 states and for 57 different insurers.Figure 3shows slightly fewer applications are submitted in the 2 years before the election. However, there is no obvious clumping right after election years, so insurers do not appear to be timing election cycles.4On the other hand, we see about a 25% drop in requests granted right before election year, suggesting that regulators are strongly affected by election cycles. Rate increase applications occur fairly infrequently: the average insurer applies for an increase about once every 4 years.5

Looking at panel A ofTable 1, the average premium increase requested is 35%; and roughly half of premium increase requests are approved in the same year. Application wait times, or the number of months between when applications are submitted and when they received a response from a regulator, are relatively short and average about half a year. Most applications are approved in fewer than 7 months (see column 6 “75th percentile”) and less than 10% of all applications are delayed past one year. Importantly, regulators often do not approve the full increase amount requested. On average, they approve an increase that is about 71% of the requested size.

Next, we collect average premiums and claims data from Form 5 of all life insurers’ National Association of Insurance Commissioner (NAIC) Long-term Care Experience Reports between 1997 to 2015. All insurance companies which sold policies in the preceding year must submit this regulatory filing, which allows a comprehensive view of LTC premiums and claims. Each observation corresponds to a company, year, and state; it includes total premiums and claims, expected total premiums and claims, as well as total lives in force. From this data, we also calculate the average premium per person for companyiin yeartand statesas:total premiumsitstotal lives in forceits⁠. Expected claims by state were only reported in the first version of the Long-term Care Experience Reports, published between 1997 and 2008, but all other variables are observed for the full sample. Panel B ofTable 1shows that the average premium per person in our sample was $1,789 per year, but there is a significant amount of variation, as demonstrated by the 25th and 75th percentiles, which are $1,219 and $2,077 respectively. Over time, average premiums are increasing by about 4% per year over our sample.

We hand-collect political data on election cycles and voting outcomes for each state and year from 1997 to 2017 through the states’ Secretary of State election result websites. For the 12 states that elect the insurance commissioner directly, we collect the commissioners’ election years, winning candidates’ names and political parties, winning vote percentages, as well as winning vote margins. For states that appoint their insurance commissioner through their governor, we collect the governors’ election years, as well as the appointed insurance commissioners’ names and political parties. Florida, Virginia, and New Mexico appoint commissioners using a committee, so they are omitted from our election analyses. Finally, for the 11 states that have elected commissioners and also report campaign financing information on their Secretary of State websites, we collect the total stock of contributions at the beginning of each year as well as the flow of new contributions received over the year.6

Looking at panel C ofTable 1, we see that regulators are evenly divided among political parties with 40% of them identifying as Democrats. The state elections are fairly competitive; insurance regulators (or the governors who appoint them) win their elections with 57.8% of the votes on average, earning about 19% more votes than the runner-up.

Lastly, if an insurer finds LTCI profits to be untenable in a state, they can choose to stop selling new policies in that state at any time, which we call insurer dropout. We cannot simply use the absence of a LTCI Experience Report to proxy for insurer dropout, because companies still file as long as they continue to service existing policies. While insurers report the number of new policies sold in their Experience Reports, this is only available at the national level and not by state. Thus, to compare supply outcomes across states, we approximate state-level insurer dropouts using advertisement filings from the NAIC System for Electronic Rates & Forms Filing database. In states in which insurance companies are required to file for advertising in order to sell insurance, we record a company as dropped if it stops filing for advertising. This dropout data covers 33 states between 2005 and 2014, and it is summarized in panel D ofTable 1.7The LTCI market has severely shrunk, and most companies drop out of their states by the end of our sample; 71% of our observations in the cross-section contain a dropout. The exact year of dropout varies and appears evenly spaced throughout time.

3.1 Subsample for applications matched on company, policy, year, and increase size

In the main text, we estimate baseline regressions on a smaller, matched subset of the application data. To cleanly identify regulatory frictions, we want to control for all unobservables of the insurance policy itself, such as the cost of providing LTC services, which would naturally comove with premium changes.8In an ideal world, we would randomize the political incentives facing the regulator and compare their responses to otherwise identical premium increase requests. Fortunately, we can observe a natural experiment similar to this, because many companies sell the same policy (e.g., Genworth Life’s Policy 7032, an individual policy for home care only that is tax-qualified and first sold in 1997) in multiple states; furthermore, thesame insureroften requestsidentical premium increasesfor thesame policyat thesame timefromdifferent states.

Different state regulators will likely be at different stages of their election cycles, have different levels of political capital, and have different political affiliations. By comparing how different states’ regulators respond to otherwise nearly identical requests, we remove actuarial considerations of the insurance product and make it easier to identify political frictions.

To create this subset, we create matched groups of applications, each indexed byj, based on having the same company, policy, year, and increase amount requested. For example, matched groupj= 3753 contains nine different applications made by Genworth Life for Policy 7032 in 2010 to the following states: Connecticut, West Virginia, Washington, Wyoming, Mississippi, Montana, Ohio, South Carolina, and Arkansas. All of these applications asked for an 18% premium increase. Any groups containing just one application, which could not be matched with any other applications, is dropped from this subsample, reducing the number of observations to roughly a quarter of the full application data set. Panel B ofTable 2summarizes key variables for our matched subset, and panel A ofTable 2summarizes key variables for the remaining applications that were submitted to only one state and were thus dropped from the matched subsample. Finally, panel C conductst-tests of sample means, testing the null hypothesis that applications to only one state (which we call unmatched) are identical to applications sent to multiple states (which we call matched) for each of our key covariates.

In our matched group subset, we observe 7,780 different applications covering 1,077 different matched groups, each containing at least two applications and up to 39 applications.9On average, we observe 7,780/1,077=7.22 different rate applications per matched group, representing submissions to 7–8 different states with different election cycles, but all during the same year. This gives us enough observations within a group to capture not only varying election cycles but also heterogeneity in political capital, political party, and campaign contributions.

Compared to the sample of unmatched applications (see panel C ofTable 2) applications in the matched sample are quite similar- on average, they request the same premium increase, receive the same fraction of the requested increase, and are equally likely to be approved. Comparing the activity of the insurers, however, insurers in the unmatched sample have more customers (2,500 more lives covered in the state), charge a lower premium (US$150 less) and hold a larger market share (1.22% bigger, based on total premiums collected in a state and year). One possible explanation for these observed differences is that the matched sample, by construction, contains insurers that must spread their resources across multiple states. Thus, if there are company-level constraints, then compared to their single-state counterparts, they may commit fewer resources and are less competitive inside each state.

To preview our main finding that political frictions, such as election cycles, affect the LTCI market by affecting regulators’ behavior, we break down the matched subsample of applications by those who submitted in the year right before election (panel A ofTable 3) versus those who submitted in all other years (panel B ofTable 3). Panel C conductst-tests of sample means, testing the null hypothesis that variables are the same in the year before election as in all other years. We see that applications submitted just before elections, when regulators feel election pressure most strongly, have a smaller probability of being approved in that year (5.95% lower on average), and they also receive a lower percentage of the requested amount (3.87% lower on average). Interestingly, applications made in the year before election are equally likely to be approved in the long run, suggesting that political frictions fade after the election year passes.

Lastly, requests made right before elections tend to ask for larger amounts (Table 3, panel C, column 1) and come from larger companies (columns 6 and 7), suggesting that companies may be aware of and respond strategically to election cycles. This pattern may arise if(1)companies that need bigger premium increases cannot afford to wait until after the election, so overall, we observe larger requests during election year, or(2)companies request more than needed during election years, anticipating that they will be approved for a smaller fraction of the request. To account for the second case of strategic behavior, we only consider multistate applications (which are harder to time) with the exact same requested amount in our matched analyses. Furthermore, when we conduct additional analyses using all applications, we examine both the total increase amount granted and the fraction of the request amount that was ultimately granted.

Overall, preliminary evidence shows that regulators feel election cycle frictions and become tougher on requests leading up to election year. In Section 4.1, we test this pattern as well as the effect of other political factors more carefully.

3.2 Tests using the full sample of applications

Insurers that apply for premium increases in multiple states may differ from insurers that apply in just one state (seeTable 2), so our findings using the matched subsample may not have external validity in the context of the full LTCI market. To directly address this, in theInternet Appendix, we repeat all of the analyses presented in the main text of the paper using the full set of applications, including both matched applicationsandunmatched applications submitted to just one state. While we can no longer control for the matched groups in these regressions, we instead control for unobservable supply shocks (e.g., there are sudden changes to the company’s supply chain) using company x year fixed effects; and we control for unobserved demand markets (e.g., a company has different consumer demographics across states) with company x state fixed effects.

Ultimately, using the full sample of applications, we find results that align with that of the matched group analyses. This gives us confidence that our findings can be generalized to the overall LTCI market, and they suggest that the matched subsample analyses do not suffer from significant sample bias.
article.4 Main Findings:
4.1 Regulatory responses to matched applications

First, we study how insurance regulators with different political climates respond to nearly identical premium increase requests within a matched groupj. Each group of applications is matched by having the same company, policy, year, and requested increase amount. More details on the matched group data construction can be found in Section 3.1.

4.1.1 Election cycles

First, we hypothesize that as election year approaches, the insurance regulator becomes less likely to approve a premium increase request. Since insurance regulators are generally either elected or appointed by the governor (who is elected), this may arise for several reasons: price hikes are unfavorable to her constituents, her attention is focused more on the election campaign, or other political considerations that correlate with elections, such as the workload of office staff. In all of these cases, we would expect that the regulator approves fewer requests as she approaches her election year (if directly elected) or her governor’s election year (if appointed).10Furthermore, if she is particularly worried about the unpopularity of large rate hikes among voters, then she may also approve smaller-sized increases as election year approaches.11

InTable 4, we test whether nearly identical premium increase requests submitted to different states are treated differently depending on how close their insurance regulator is to election year. For each matched group of applicationsjand states, we estimate the following regressions:

The outcomes of interest include whether the application is approved (Equation (1)) and the fraction of the requested increase that was approved (Equation (2)).12Xjsis a vector of variables that control for the company and policy’s past history, including the total number of previous applications submitted, as well as the total amount of previous rate increases granted. Anecdotally, regulators aim to grant increases that are one-time only. Thus, by controlling for past increases, we can account for the fact that policies are less likely to receive more increases if they already received one in the past. Finally, we include a fixed effectαjfor each matched group (a set of applications with the same policy, company, year, and request amount) andγsfor each state.

Our main variable of interest is the number of years left in term, which is equal to one right before election and four right after election. If regulators become tougher on requests before election, we would expectββ1>0⁠. We cluster standard errors at the matched group to account for the fact that matching is used in combination with regression” (Abadie and Spiess 2022), and to account for correlations introduced by the sampling procedure (Abadie et al. 2023). While matching allows us to control for important and otherwise unobservable traits of the LTCI product (e.g., policy benefits, tax treatment, and customer demographics), the trade-off is that the sampling procedure mechanically drops all unmatched applications, of which there is a substantial portion. Clustering at the matched group level thus allows us to consider that there are clusters in the population of interest that are not represented in our sample, and it allows the error terms to be correlated within each matched group.

InTable 4, as expected, we find that regulators are less likely to approve premium increase requests as well as grant smaller increases when they approach election year.13Rregressions reported in columns 1, 3, and 5 match requests just on policy and year; and regressions reported in columns 2, 4, and 6 match requests on the requested amount as well.14Overall, results are very similar across these specifications, so for all future analyses, we focus on the tightest identification, which matches on policy, year, company, and amount. Results reported in columns 1 and 2 examine probability of approval in the same year, while results reported in columns 3 and 4 examine probability of approval across all years. We see that the results are much stronger for same-year approvals, suggesting that the election cycle effect wanes over time. In subsequent analyses, to capture the immediate effect of elections, we focus on the probability of approval in the same year.

More specifically, starting with column 2 ofTable 4, we see that as the regulator moves from the year before election to the year after election, she is 3*2.20 or 6.54 pp more likely to approve a premium increase request in the same year. This is roughly a 13% increase compared to the unconditional average of 50.4 pp.Figure 4, panel A, illustrates the positive and linear relationship between the probability that a regulator approves a request and her years left in term.15In column 4 ofTable 4, we see that election cycle pressures relax in the long run; requests submitted just after election are only 2.13 pp more likely to be approved compared to the year before.

Finally, in column 6, we also see that as the regulator moves to the year after election, she approves 2.91 pp (or 4.1%) more of the requested amount. Taken altogether, these findings are consistent and show that regulators are more sensitive to premium increases shortly before election cycles. One potential explanation for this pattern is that they are worried about getting votes, and price increases are unpopular among their constituents. If so, then regulators who have more support from voters prior to the election should be less worried about winning the election, and thus, face smaller election cycle frictions. In the next section, we test whether election cycle frictions can be ameliorated by political capital, as proxied for by prior winning vote margins and tenure length.

4.1.2 Political capital

Not all regulators feel the same amount of election cycle pressure, and we next hypothesize that regulators with higher political capital should be less affected by local election cycles.16Since political capital cannot be directly observed, we capture it indirectly in two ways: either using the winning vote margin, calculated as the winning vote share minus the runner-up’s vote share from the last election; or by the duration of the regulators’ stay in office. Our reasoning for the first is that if the regulator wins by a larger margin, she feels less threatened by competing candidates and more secure in her position.17Our reasoning for the second is that if the regulator stays in the same position for many years, she will gain confidence through prior experience, which makes her less likely to worry about political opposition.

To test this, using the sample of directly elected regulators inTables 5and6, we run the following regressions for matched groupjand states:

β β β β γ α ϵ approval j s = β 1 Years left in term j s + β 2 capital j s + β 3 ( capital j s ) × ( Years left in term j s ) + β 4 X j s + γ s + α j + ϵ j s (3)

β β approved / requested j s = β 1 Years left in term j s + β 2 capital j s

β β γ α ϵ + β 3 ( capital j s ) × ( years left in term j s ) + β 4 X j s + γ s + α j + ϵ j s (4)

Looking at columns 2 and 5 ofTable 5, we see that regulators with higher voter approval are indeed less sensitive to election cycles. The result is significant at the 99% confidence level for approval in same year (column 2) and the percent of request approved (column 5). The size of the effect is substantial; for example, in column 2, a one-standard-deviation increase in winning margin (18.2%) would lower the effect of election cycles by 29% or 13.2 pp on average. To control for unobservable characteristics of the regulator that might influence voting margins as well as premium increase decisions, such as her past history of premium increase approvals, we add regulator fixed effects on top of state fixed effects in columns 3 and 6. Comparing the same regulator in years in which she is more popular with her constituents to other years, we find that she becomes less sensitive to election cycles. Compared to the baseline in columns 2 and 5, the results are smaller but overall very similar.

Looking at columns 2 and 5 ofTable 6, we see that regulators that have been in office longer are also less sensitive to election cycles. The findings are generally strong and consistently significant at the 99% confidence level. The only exception is in column 2, where we find that tenure accentuates election cycle pressures. However, this is likely due to unobservable qualities of the regulator, because once we control for the regulator fixed effect in column 3, we find that when the same regulator stays longer in office, they become less sensitive to election cycles. Holding the regulator fixed, we estimate that each additional term, or four additional years of tenure, would lower the effect of election cycles by 1.76 (column 3) to 2.48 pp (column 6). These differences are economically meaningful, representing 3.9% and 3.8% of the unconditional average, respectively.

Overall,Tables 5and6combined provide strong support that regulators may become less sensitive to election cycle pressures if they have more voter support or if they have served in their roles longer.

4.1.3 Party affiliation

In addition to election cycles, party affiliation can also affect the regulator’s response to price increase requests. One of the platforms of the Democratic party is to provide affordable health care, using government interventions if necessary. As a result, Democratic regulators might be tougher on premium increase requests, leading to lower approval rates and lower magnitudes of rate increases. Our new explanatory variable,Democrat, is a dummy variable equal to one if the regulator is a Democrat, and it is zero otherwise. In columns 2 and 4 ofTable 7, we find that Democratic regulators are 11.1 pp less likely to approve a rate increase, and they approve 15.5 pp less of the requested amount on average. The economic magnitudes of these differences are quite large, several times larger than all observed variation over the election cycle in Section 4.

It is important to consider that constituents of a Democratic regulator may have different demographics than those of a Republican regulator. By law, insurers have limited ability to price demographic factors, such as gender in the LTCI market. However, if constituents differ along a priced factor, this would affect the regulator’s decision on increase requests and also bias our estimates. State-fixed effects partially control for this, but differing trends in demographics over time could still affect regulator party affiliation and insurance pricing. To address this additional concern, we next look at the short periods of time following sudden switches in political party within the same state.

In columns 2 and 4 ofTable 7, we look at the four years following a switch in party affiliation of the regulator.18We find that shortly after the state’s insurance regulator switches to a Republican, premium increase requests are 4.87 pp (or 4.87/50.4=9.7%) more likely to be approved and the approved increase is about 16 pp (or 16.0/71.5=22.4%) more of the requested.

On the other hand, when the regulator switches to a Democrat, requests are 11.3 pp (or 22.4%) less likely to be approved. To identify switches in political party that are the most unexpected, we further look at switches following close races in columns 3 and 6.19Examining close races in which the regulator won by a margin of less than 10%, results are even more dramatic. When the regulator narrowly switches to a Republican, requests are 29.7 pp (or 58.9%) more likely to be approved and increases are 28.1 pp (or 39.3%) more of the requested amount on average. Taken together, these findings are complementary and demonstrate that regulators affiliated with the Democratic party are overall tougher on premium increase requests.

4.1.4 Campaign contributions

As politicians, insurance regulators depend on contributions to finance their campaigns, often raising their funds from insurance companies and affiliated agents.20For example, in 2003, when Robert Wooley ran for Louisiana insurance commissioner, six of his largest contributions (each for the maximum allotment of $5,000) came from Allied Specialty Insurance and its related agencies (Louisiana 2003). In 2014, when Ralph Hudgens ran for Georgia insurance commissioner, 35 campaign contributions came from insurance-related agencies totaling $28,700. They included donations from Waites and Foshee, AmTrust, MAG Mutual, Safeway, Senior Life, as well as donations by executives from Farmers, Blue Cross Blue Shield, United Automobile Insurance, and CIGNA (Georgia 2014). Furthermore, some industry donations to regulators have been specifically related to applications requiring the regulators’ approval. For example, in 2018, California commissioner Ricardo Lara accepted contributions from several donors tied to Applied Underwriters, a workers’ compensation insurer, just one month before the insurer needed approval from Lara to sell the company (McDonald 2019).

If regulators depend on industry donations to finance their campaigns, then they may become more lenient on insurers’ requests when they need more funding. Next, we test whether campaign contributions are generally related to regulator behavior by estimating the following two regressions for matched groupjand states:

β β β β γ α ϵ approval j s = β 1 Years left in term j s + β 2 new contributions j s + β 3 previous contributions j s + β 4 X j s + γ s + α j + ϵ j s (7)

β β β β γ α ϵ approved / requested j s = β 1 Years left in term j s + β 2 new contributions j s + β 3 previous contributions j s + β 4 X j s + γ s + α j + ϵ j s (8)

The outcome, control, and explanatory variableYears left in termare the same as before.New contributionsis the amount of new contributions which was raised over the course of the year, andPrevious contributionsis the total amount of cash accumulated before the start of the year. If regulators are more lenient in order to receive more contributions from industry, thenββ2>0⁠; and if wealthier regulators do not need to raise as many donations from industry, thenββ3<0⁠.

Table 8shows that, as predicted, regulators who receive a higher inflow of campaign funds are also more lenient on premium increase requests that year, although the effect is very small. In column 3, we see that a regulator who raises US$100,000 more over the year is 0.27 pp (or .27/45.1 = 0.60%) more likely to approve an application and approves increases that are 0.19 pp (or .19/63.81=0.30%) more of the request on average. The small magnitudes likely reflect the fact that most campaign funds are unrelated to LTCI companies. While we do not know how much of the campaign funds come directly from industry, the observed pattern is consistent with the hypothesis that some of the same-year contributions may come from corporations in exchange for a favor from the regulator.

4.1.5 Identification concerns and external validity

So far, we rely on a matching approach to find groups of premium increase applications that are as similar as possible, except for the state regulators that review them. To have unbiased estimates of political frictions, we need to assume no omitted variables are correlated with both the independent variable (e.g., years until next election) and the outcome variable (e.g., probability of approval).

While we try to control for most unobservable covariates by matching on company and policy, the same exact policy and company may still attract different kinds of customers in different states. Thus, arguably, the most worrisome omitted variable is the possibility of different consumer markets across states within a company, which may cause some insurance to be more mispriced and in need of a bigger increase than others. In the matched subsample, the median insurer applies to the same state in only two different years (mean is 2.4 years), so directly adding company x state fixed effects would be untenable as they would remove about half of all variation in our data. Matching groups on the size of the requested rate increase, which presumably correlates with the size of mispricing, helps reduce this concern.

In addition to matching on exact request size, two additional considerations address the concern of biased estimates from omitting local consumer demand variables:

Local consumer demand factors are probably not correlated with most of our explanatory variables, such as states’ election cycles or close switches in political affiliation of the regulator. If they are not correlated, then omitting them only adds noise to our estimates, but our findings remain unbiased. In the case of campaign contributions, however, they remain a potential concern. For example, Genworth may make more contributions to Alabama’s regulator, if they have more lucrative business in that state. This could influence both the dependent variable (i.e., regulator’s campaign contributions) and outcome variable (i.e., the regulator’s probability of approval).In theInternet AppendixSection A, we reestimate all the analyses in Section 4.1 using the full sample of applications and include company x state fixed effects. Since the median insurer applies to the same state in six different years (mean is 6.3 years) in this sample, there is enough variation to identify our results. If the matched subsample were truly biased, then we would expect the analyses repeated using the full sample to produce contrasting estimates. Reassuringly, however, we see that the coefficients are similar and the findings generally consistent across both sets of analyses.

In addition to omitted variables, one might worry about external validity and the broader economic magnitudes of the political frictions shown in Section 4.1. Three potential related issues are worth noting. First,conditionalon applying for a price increase, LTCI policies face political frictions; however, are these applications commonplace enough to affect aggregate pricing trajectories across the country? Second, some evidence indicates that companies strategically ask for bigger increases before elections (seeTable 3, panel C). If companies can anticipate election cycles and adjust applications accordingly, then are realized LTCI premiums still affected by political frictions? Finally, due to the matching process, we estimate our findings using a small subsample of companies and policies. Would our findings change if we were to consider the full national sample with all companies and policies?

To address these concerns, in the next Section 4.2, we study the effect of election cycles on a new data set comprised ofrealized average premiumsreported byall LTCI companiesto the NAIC. To preview the findings, companies in states that go from an election year to the year after an election experience, on average, experience a 3*0.29 = 0.87-pp bigger increase in annual premiums.21This difference is statistically significant and meaningfully represents roughly 20% of the unconditional increase of 4.24 pp.

In sum, we find that political frictions found in granular regulatory applications ultimately affect real-world pricing trajectories across the full LTCI market. Furthermore, even if there is strategic behavior by insurers, it does not appear to effectively offset the impact of election cycles on aggregate premiums.

Finally, companies applying to many states at once may not be able to time all relevant election cycles, but companies applying to just one state may be much more strategic. Thus, we may be concerned that the frictions found in the matched subsample may be much weaker when we zoom out and consider the full picture. To address this, Table A1 in theInternet Appendixrepeats the matched analysis for the full sample of rate applications, and it looks at both the unscaled level of increase granted and the fraction of the requested increase granted. If strategic behavior by insurers causes some companies to artificially ask for more right before an election, then we would only find an election-cycle effect in percentage terms (column 2), but not in the raw levels (column 1). However, we find a significant effect of elections in both, reassuring us that insurers’ strategic behavior does not appear to fully eliminate political frictions.

4.2 Aggregate premium changes

So far, we have seen that political incentives surrounding the regulator can cause her to not approve or approve smaller amounts on premium increase applications. Yet, it remains unclear whether these individual decisions on applications add up to affect nationwide pricing patterns. In this section, we provide evidence that political frictions, as measured by election cycles, can also explain aggregate premium movements across the country. This also validates our prior results by showing that election cycle patterns hold over the entire U.S. LTCI market.22

Using nationwide premiums data, for companyiin statesand yeart, we estimate the following baseline regression equation:

β α α α ϵ premium change ist = β 1 years left in term s t + α i + α s + α t + ϵ ist (9)

The dependent variable ispremium change, the year-over-year percent change in average premiums per person, which is calculated as total premiums divided by total lives in force. The main explanatory variable,years left in term, is the number of years left until the next election year.αi,αs, andαtare company, state, and year fixed effects respectively. To further control for unobservable trends, we also add company x year and company x state fixed effects in some specifications. Since the timing of election cycles is arguably orthogonal to most LTCI pricing factors, ifββ1>0⁠, it would provide evidence that political pressures affect aggregate market prices.

As a preview,Figure 4, panel B, shows a binscatter plot with the number of years until election year on thex-axis and the average year-over-year change in premiums per person on they-axis, controlling for state and year fixed effects. We see that, very similar to our findings in the application data, the relationship between years left in term and premium changes is generally linear and negative.

Next, we statistically test this relationship inTable 9, where we estimate the regression inEquation (9). In column 1, we only include state and year fixed effects, and we add company fixed effects in column 2. To interpret theβ1coefficient of 0.29 in column 2, we move from the year before election (years left in term=1) to the year after election (years left in term=4) and find an average rate increase of 3*0.29, or 0.87 pp. This finding is economically meaningful because it is roughly one-fifth of the baseline premium change in our sample of 4.2 pp. Columns 3–5 tighten our identification further by controlling for unobserved shocks with either company x state fixed effects (column 3), company x year fixed effects (column 4), or both (column 5). The results remain consistent in size and significant at the 95% confidence level across all specifications, suggesting strong evidence that election cycle frictions are strong enough to affect aggregate pricing patterns in the U.S. LTCI market.

We consider three proxies for firm performance gathered from NAIC filings, each measured at the firm, state, and year levels: average premium collected (panel A), average cash flow (panel B), and average number of lives covered (panel C). We define average premium as total premiums collected divided by total lives covered, and we define average cash flow as the total amount of premiums collected minus the total amount of claims paid out, divided by total lives covered.

The key coefficients of interest areββt−1throughββt−3⁠, corresponding to the main explanatory variables: the percent of premium requests approved over the last 3 years.23Since a company may not apply at all in a given year (in which case the percent approved is mechanically zero), we control for this by adding a dummy variable equal to one if the company applied for a rate increase. Finally, we control for baseline differences in customer demographics using company x state fixed effects (all columns) and examine within a local market by controlling for state x year fixed effects (columns 4–6). A priori, we expect that more competitive firms will have higher premiums, larger cash flows, and more lives covered.

Looking atTable 10and focusing within a local market (columns 4 through 6), we see that when regulators approve more requests for a company in a state and year, the company becomes significantly more competitive via higher average premiums (panel A) and cash flows (panel B), but also covers fewer lives (panel C) compared to its local competitors. Looking at column 3 of panels A and B, this effect cannot be measured by looking at the next year alone. The sign is actually reversed, indicating that there is some time between when a rate increase is applied for and when it begins to take effect. When we expand our window and look to the next 3 years in column 6, we see that successful rate requests lead to significantly higher premiums and cash flows for their firms compared to local competitors. Interestingly, this effect is generally stronger with time, suggesting that local competitors are not easily able to catch up. Since higher prices mean that more policyholders are likely to stop paying premiums and allow their policies to lapse, it is not surprising that we also observe fewer lives covered. Since this negative effect stays strong over time, it suggests that the company does not sell enough new policies to offset the customers lost from existing policies.

Overall, we find suggestive evidence that rate application outcomes affect the competitiveness firms within their local markets. Successful applications give insurers an edge over their competitors when it comes to premiums and cash flows, but they can also lead insurers to lose customers. These effects persist for at least the next 3 years, suggesting that competitors cannot easily catch up by applying for another rate increase. Our findings are supported by anecdotal evidence that regulators do not like to grant multiple increases for any given product. Also, rate increase applications appear to be costly to prepare as each company, on average, only applies every 4 years.

4.3.2 Insurer cash reserves

While firms earn more cash in the short run following a successful rate application, how does this affect the buildup of their cash holdings in the long run? Next, we study an aspect of firm performance often cited by insurers when leaving a state: cash reserves, or cumulative cash flows over time.24Both smaller approved increases and delays in approval can lead to permanently weakened reserves. For example, if companies receive a smaller increase than desired, this leads to a permanent shortfall in annual cash flows. Over time, this adds up to significantly lower reserves and a higher likelihood of dropout. Even if companies receive the requested increase, but with a delay, this leads to a shortfall in annual cash flows during the years of delay. Cumulatively, this still leads to permanently depressed cash reserves and a higher likelihood of dropout.

First, we test whether companies operating in states with high election cycle frictions have lower cash reserves over time, compared to those operating in states with low election cycle frictions. To do this, we rank the size of states’ election cycle frictions by estimating the following equation separately for each states:

β ϵ Increase approved ist = β s Years left in term s t + ϵ ist . (11)

Each observation corresponds to one companyiand yeart. The outcome variable of interest isIncrease approved, which is the average premium increase granted by the regulator. The key explanatory variable,Years left in term, is the number of years left until the next election year. All 50 of our regression estimates are reported in theInternet Appendix, where each row corresponds to a separate state, and high friction states are defined as the half of states with the highest estimates ofβs.25In general, we see that the coefficient forYears left in termvaries a lot across states, and their average estimated effect is 0.77, which is bigger but similar to the nationwide coefficient of 0.47 estimated inTable 9.

AsFigure 5shows, the difference between states with high election frictions and states with low election frictions is economically large. Companies in high friction states earn roughly 75% of the cash flows of companies from low friction states; over our sample of 14 years, this adds up to a gap in reserves of roughly $2.39 billion. Since cumulative premiums in low friction states total $16.6 billion, if we hold total claims constant, the observed gap would represent a 14% difference in total premiums. In reduced form estimates of election cycles on premium changes (regression outcomes by state are shown in theInternet Appendix), high friction states haveβscoefficients of 2%–3%, which translates to a 6%–9% gap in total premiums following an election year. If we also allow claims to vary, then this difference in premiums may translate into an even wider gap in cash flows, because higher premiums may cause greater consumer lapse rates and thus even lower claims.

Finally, in panel B ofFigure 5, we see that companies operating in states with Democratic regulators accumulated significantly less cash than those operating in states with Republican regulators. In particular, companies with Democratic regulators earned roughly 60% of the cash flows of companies with Republican regulators, adding up to a $4 billion gap in reserves over 14 years. Since cumulative premiums collected by Republican states totaled $19.5 billion over our sample, this roughly represents a 20% gap in total premiums holding claims constant. This is similar to the reduced form estimates of 16 and 28% in columns 5 and 6 ofTable 7. Overall, while party affiliation is only one potential determinant of regulator stringency, these findings suggest that regulators have a significant impact on company earnings, and as a result, can affect company dropouts. We will further explore this in Section 4.4.

4.4 Insurer dropout

Finally, we conclude by showing that political frictions can explain the decision of insurers to stop supplying insurance or to drop out of the market. We preview the results by plotting the probability of insurer dropout as a function of either the average fraction of requested increase that was eventually approved (Figure 6, panel A) or the percent of all premium increase requests eventually approved (Figure 6, panel B). Overall, we see that when regulators are tougher on premium increase applications, companies are more likely to drop out. This is not surprising, as we have seen that restrictions in premium increases decrease the performance of insurers in Section 4.3.1, and over time, dampen insurers’ cash reserves in Section 4.3.2. As a result, insurers may worry about their ability to weather future cost shocks and ultimately choose to drop out of the local LTCI market.

Similar to Section 4.1.3, to help alleviate endogeneity concerns, we also examine sudden switches in political party within a state. We test whether company dropouts are serially correlated with election cycles by testing ifββ1>0⁠, and we test whether companies are more likely to drop out under a Democratic regulator by testing ifββ2>0⁠.

In columns 1 and 2 ofTable 12, we test the univariate effect of election cycles and Democratic regulators respectively. In column 3, we look at years in which the party associated with the regulator switched to Democratic or Republican; and finally, in column 4, we combine all of the factors together. Examining the full regression specification in column 4 ofTable 12, we find that companies are 10.3 pp more likely to drop out under a Democratic regulator, but we find no significant effect of election cycles on company dropouts in the next year.

Since election cycles are short and occur regularly, their effects on insurer dropouts likely accumulate over long periods of time rather than over the short span of a year. Our findings support this: the coefficient forDemocratis negative and larger than the positive coefficient forChg to Democrat. This implies that companies areeven more likely to drop outafter they have been regulated by a Democratic commissioner for many years. More precisely, companies are 10.1 pp more likely to drop out of a state if they had a Democratic regulator for more than 4 years, and they are 10.1-4.4=5.7 pp more likely to drop out of a state if they just switched to a Democratic regulator within the last 4 years.

Overall, these findings show that political factors like party affiliation directly affect companies’ decisions to supply LTCI, but it may take many years for these effects to accumulate and have a significant impact.
article.5 Conclusion:
Novel financial products are difficult to price, and they present significant regulatory challenges. The long-term care insurance (LTCI) market is relatively new, and it contains high cost uncertainty due to the long-run nature of the product. Its pricing difficulties have led to repeated negotiations between insurance companies and regulators, which in turn have affected the ability of insurers to make profits. Examining these interactions over the last two decades, we find novel evidence that political frictions due to the regulator significantly affected prices as well as supply in the LTCI market.

Using detailed data on premium increase requests submitted by insurers to the regulator, we isolate the effect of the regulator on prices and show how it varies depending on several political factors. Comparing premium increase requests submitted by the same company in the same year for the same product to different states, we find that regulators are 13% more likely to approve a premium increase request following an election year. Furthermore, Republican regulators are 9.7% more likely to grant increases and grant 22.4% more of the requested amount compared to other regulators. Over time, these differences in pricing can accumulate to have a large impact on insurer cash flows and, consequently, affect insurers’ decisions to supply insurance. For example, we find that companies operating in states with Democratic regulators only earn 60% of the cash flows of their counterparts with Republican regulators. Ultimately, we find lower premium increases are correlated with higher rates of insurer dropout.

Our analyses have implications for the millions of Americans entering old age who must find funding for the high costs of LTC, as well as valuable lessons from the LTCI market that could be extended to other settings. For instance, our findings suggest that a nationally organized, rotating committee of regulators with staggered appointment dates can attenuate political pressures and potentially improve novel financial markets. Some examples of novel markets that exist today and are growing over time include subsidized plans under the Affordable Care Act (ACA) and hybrid annuity products seeking to replace LTCI.

21 Compared to the application data, where the average request amount is 35%, the 0.87-pp magnitude of change is remarkably similar (from column 6 of Table 4 : 3*0.97*.35 = 1.02pp). This does not take into account, however, the fact that applications are also less likely to receive an approval closer to election year.

22 While an insurer only shows up in our application-level data set if it sold LTCI between 2007 and 2017 in California, all LTCI companies are represented in the average premiums data, which we gathered from NAIC LTCI Experience Forms.

23 In the Internet Appendix , we repeat the analysis using the size of the increase rather than the percent approved, and we find qualitatively similar results.

24 We define cash flows in the same way as Section 4.3: total premiums collected minus total claims paid out. Our measure of cash flows does not control for differences in policies, so we cannot directly interpret them as profits. Generally speaking, larger cash flows are associated with higher profits. Most importantly, companies with more cash flows have larger cash reserves, which allows them to have a greater buffer against future cost shocks. Conversely, insufficient reserves may lead to great losses for insurers in the future, causing them to exit the market.

25 In case some states’ point estimates are noisily estimated, we also sort on the t -statistics rather than the coefficients, and we find nearly identical results (see Internet Appendix ).

26 To track insurer dropout over time, we use advertising applications as a proxy. In the Internet Appendix , however, we eliminate the time dimension and use a direct but nationwide measure of company dropout. Our estimated regression confirms that regulators’ decisions can also explain dropout patterns in the cross-section.
